title: Editing reproducible documents
text:
Outline

What reproducible means in practice.
Quarto vs R Markdown вҖ” one shared engine, two dialects.
YAML front-matter: metadata, output formats, execution options.
Chunk options: echo, eval, cache, fig-cap, tbl-cap.
Publication-ready tables with gt and knitr::kable().
Export: HTML, PDF (via LaTeX), Word.

title: Editing reproducible documents
text:
Anatomy of a Quarto document
---
title: "Tourism in the Canary Islands, 2024"
author: "Your Name"
date: last-modified
format:
  html:
    toc: true
  pdf: default
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
---

# Introduction
Regular prose. Markdown **works** as expected.

# Data
```{r}
library(dplyr)
occupancy |> summarise(mean(nights))
```

title: Editing reproducible documents
text:
Chunk options youвҖҷll use daily



Option
What it does




echo: false
hide the code, keep the output


eval: false
show the code, donвҖҷt run it


include: false
run silently, hide everything


fig-cap: "..."
figure caption (cross-referable)


tbl-cap: "..."
table caption


cache: true
skip re-runs until the chunk changes

title: Editing reproducible documents
Editing reproducible documents
text:
Rendering
quarto render report.qmd              # to default (html)
quarto render report.qmd --to pdf     # to PDF
quarto render report.qmd --to docx    # to Word

title: Editing reproducible documents
text:
Best practices

One .qmd per analysis вҖ” not one mega-notebook.
Keep raw data untouched; scripts transform it into intermediate datasets.
Use here::here() so your report renders on any machine.
Commit the .qmd, not the rendered .html вҖ” the CI builds it.

title: One-variable visualisation
text:
Outline

Why visualise before you model.
Distribution at a glance: histograms, density, rug plots.
Robust alternatives: boxplots and violin plots.
Categorical: bars, lollipop, waffle.
From base R to ggplot2: the grammar of graphics.

title: One-variable visualisation
section: Base R В· quick and local
text:
Base R В· quick and local

hist(hotels$price,
     breaks = 20,
     col    = "#0067a2",
     main   = "Distribution of nightly price",
     xlab   = "Price (вӮ¬)")

title: One-variable visualisation
text:
ggplot2 В· the grammar

library(ggplot2)

ggplot(hotels, aes(x = price)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 30, fill = "#0067a2", alpha = 0.85) +
  labs(title = "Distribution of nightly price",
       x = "Price (вӮ¬)", y = "Hotels") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12)

title: One-variable visualisation
text:
Histogram vs density

ggplot(hotels, aes(x = price)) +
  geom_histogram(aes(y = after_stat(density)),
                 bins = 30, fill = "#0067a2", alpha = 0.55) +
  geom_density(linewidth = 1) +
  theme_minimal()

title: One-variable visualisation
text:
Boxplot В· robust summary

ggplot(hotels, aes(y = price)) +
  geom_boxplot(fill = "#f4f3ee") +
  coord_flip() +
  theme_minimal()

title: One-variable visualisation
One-variable visualisation
text:
Rules of thumb

One variable вҶ’ one plot type: continuous вҶ’ histogram/density; categorical вҶ’ bar chart.
Order matters for categories вҖ” reorder by frequency or by value.
Minimise ink; use theme_minimal() (or theme_void() for maps).
Titles, units, source line вҖ” always.

title: Data wrangling with the tidyverse
text:
Outline

The pipe |> and the tidyverse grammar.
dplyr verbs: filter, select, mutate, arrange, summarise, group_by.
Reshaping with tidyr: pivot_longer, pivot_wider, separate, unite.
Joining tables: left_join, inner_join, anti_join.
Missing values and type coercion gotchas.

title: Data wrangling with the tidyverse
Data wrangling with the tidyverse
text:
The six dplyr verbs



Verb
Purpose




filter()
keep rows that match a condition


select()
keep / drop columns


mutate()
add or transform columns


arrange()
sort rows


summarise()
collapse to one row per group


group_by()
set the grouping context

title: Data wrangling with the tidyverse
section: Group-wise summaries В· Eurostat nights spent

title: Data wrangling with the tidyverse
text:
Wide vs long В· pivot_longer()

library(tidyr)

nights_wide <- nights |>
  pivot_wider(names_from = year, values_from = value)

nights_wide |>
  pivot_longer(cols = `2019`:`2024`,
               names_to = "year", values_to = "value")

title: Data wrangling with the tidyverse
text:
Joins В· occupancy + capacity

occupancy |>
  left_join(capacity, by = c("island", "year", "month")) |>
  mutate(occupancy_rate = nights / available_beds)

Know your joins:

left_join() вҖ” keep every row of the left table.
inner_join() вҖ” keep only matches.
anti_join() вҖ” rows from left without a match on the right.

title: Data wrangling with the tidyverse
text:
NA handling

x <- c(2, NA, 7, 4)
mean(x)                # NA

[1] NA

mean(x, na.rm = TRUE)  # 4.33

[1] 4.333333

sum(is.na(x))          # count NAs

[1] 1

title: Data wrangling with the tidyverse
text:
Recap

Pipe |> chains verbs, reads left-to-right.
group_by() |> summarise() is the bread and butter of reporting.
Always pivot when a column stores values, not variables.

title: Data in R
text:
Outline

R, RStudio, Quarto, tidyverse вҖ” what each is for.
R as a calculator В· expressions, assignment, functions.
Atomic types В· numeric, character, logical, factor, date.
Vectors В· creation, arithmetic, subsetting.
Data frames and tibbles В· the rectangular data model.
Importing tourism data В· CSV, Excel, SPSS, Eurostat API.

title: Data in R
text:
Four pieces, one stack









What it is
You writeвҖҰ




R
The language and engine.
Code that computes.


RStudio / Positron
The IDE where you work.
Nothing R-specific вҖ” itвҖҷs the editor.


Quarto
The publishing system.
Reports, slides, web pages, books.


tidyverse
A family of R packages with consistent design.
library(tidyverse) at the top of every script.



Install R from https://cloud.r-project.org, RStudio from https://posit.co/download/rstudio-desktop/.

title: Data in R
text:
The RStudio layout
в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
в”Ӯ                               в”Ӯ                          в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ   Editor                      в”Ӯ   Environment / History  в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ   (your .R or .qmd files)     в”Ӯ   (objects currently     в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ                               в”Ӯ    in memory)            в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ                               в”Ӯ                          в”Ӯ
в”ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”јв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Ө
в”Ӯ                               в”Ӯ                          в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ   Console                     в”Ӯ   Files / Plots /        в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ   (live R session)            в”Ӯ   Packages / Help        в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ                               в”Ӯ                          в”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ

Write in the editor, send a line with Ctrl + Enter (Cmd + Enter on Mac).
The console shows the output.
Files pane is your file browser; Git pane appears when the folder is a repo.

title: Data in R
section: Projects В· always work inside an .Rproj
text:
Projects В· always work inside an .Rproj

Double-click quantitative-methods-master-tides.Rproj to open the project.
Working directory is fixed to the project root вҶ’ paths are portable.
Packages, history and environment are isolated per project.







Tip


Never run setwd("C:/Users/yourname/..."). If you see that in any tutorial, close the tab.

title: Data in R
text:
Packages В· extending R
Base R is a small kernel. Everything useful вҖ” tidyverse, plotting, model fitting, reading Excel, hitting the Eurostat API вҖ” ships as a package that you install separately from CRAN, the canonical mirror.

# install once per laptop
install.packages("tidyverse")
install.packages(c("readxl", "haven", "here", "eurostat"))

# load at the top of every script that uses them
library(tidyverse)
library(here)







Tip


install.packages() downloads and compiles вҖ” slow, but only once. library() is fast and goes at the top of every script. One single library(tidyverse) pulls in eight core packages (dplyr, ggplot2, tibble, readr, tidyr, purrr, stringr, forcats) вҖ” the workhorses for the rest of the course.

title: Data in R
text:
First expressions

2 + 2

[1] 4

17 %% 5        # modulo

[1] 2

2 ^ 10         # power

[1] 1024

sqrt(81)

[1] 9

log(exp(1))    # natural log of e

[1] 1


R evaluates each line top-to-bottom and prints the result. Nothing else happens.

title: Data in R
text:
Assignment

visitors <- 1250          # store a value in a name
nights   <- 4.2
spending <- 85

total <- visitors * nights * spending
total

[1] 446250


Use <- (Alt + - in RStudio inserts it) for assignment. Names can contain letters, digits, _ and ., but must start with a letter.

title: Data in R
text:
Functions
Functions take inputs and return an output:

round(4.2, digits = 0)

[1] 4

mean(c(2, 5, 7, 10, 14))

[1] 7.6

seq(from = 2024, to = 2026, by = 1)

[1] 2024 2025 2026








Tip


Type mean( and press F1 in RStudio to open the help page for the function. ?mean does the same from the console.

title: Data in R
text:
Getting help

?mean              # help for one function
??"linear model"   # search across packages
example(lm)        # run the documented examples

Sites you will use a lot:

https://stat.ethz.ch/R-manual/ вҖ” canonical reference.
https://r4ds.hadley.nz/ вҖ” R for Data Science, 2nd edition (free online).
Stack Overflow, tagged [r].

title: Data in R
section: The five types you meet every day
text:
The five types you meet every day

typeof(3.14)              # "double"   вҖ” real numbers

[1] "double"

typeof(1L)                # "integer"  вҖ” note the L suffix

[1] "integer"

typeof("Gran Canaria")    # "character"

[1] "character"

typeof(TRUE)              # "logical"

[1] "logical"

class(factor(c("a","b","a")))   # "factor"

[1] "factor"

class(Sys.Date())         # "Date"

[1] "Date"


We lump numeric (double + integer) together in practice.

title: Data in R
text:
Numeric

price    <- 82.50            # double
stars    <- 4L               # integer
is.numeric(price)

[1] TRUE

is.numeric(stars)

[1] TRUE

# be careful with floating-point comparisons
0.1 + 0.2 == 0.3             # FALSE!

[1] FALSE

all.equal(0.1 + 0.2, 0.3)    # TRUE

[1] TRUE

title: Data in R
text:
Character

island <- "Lanzarote"
nchar(island)

[1] 9

toupper(island)

[1] "LANZAROTE"

# paste strings together
paste("Hotel", island, sep = " В· ")

[1] "Hotel В· Lanzarote"

# case matters
"Lanzarote" == "lanzarote"

[1] FALSE

title: Data in R
text:
Logical

is_open <- TRUE
T == TRUE                # TRUE вҖ” but don't use T, it can be overwritten

[1] TRUE

sum(c(TRUE, FALSE, TRUE, TRUE))   # 3 вҖ” logicals coerce to 0/1

[1] 3


Operators return logicals:

82.50 > 70

[1] TRUE

"Lanzarote" %in% c("Tenerife", "Lanzarote", "La Palma")

[1] TRUE

title: Data in R
section: Factor В· categories with known levels
text:
Factor В· categories with known levels

island <- factor(
  c("Gran Canaria", "Tenerife", "Gran Canaria", "Lanzarote"),
  levels = c("Gran Canaria", "Tenerife", "Lanzarote",
             "Fuerteventura", "La Palma", "La Gomera", "El Hierro")
)

levels(island)

[1] "Gran Canaria"  "Tenerife"      "Lanzarote"     "Fuerteventura"
[5] "La Palma"      "La Gomera"     "El Hierro"    

table(island)

island
 Gran Canaria      Tenerife     Lanzarote Fuerteventura      La Palma 
            2             1             1             0             0 
    La Gomera     El Hierro 
            0             0 


Factors matter when the order of categories is meaningful (e.g.В low, medium, high) or when all levels should appear in a table even if some have zero observations.

title: Data in R
text:
Date and time

today <- Sys.Date()
today

[1] "2026-04-23"

class(today)

[1] "Date"

# arithmetic on dates
today + 30

[1] "2026-05-23"

as.Date("2026-06-08") - today     # days to the first exam

Time difference of 46 days

format(today, "%A, %d %B %Y")     # pretty-print

[1] "Thursday, 23 April 2026"


Use the lubridate package (inside the tidyverse) for anything more serious вҖ” weвҖҷll cover it in Data wrangling.

title: Data in R
text:
Coercion gotchas

c(1, 2, "three")      # everything becomes character

[1] "1"     "2"     "three"

c(1, 2, TRUE)         # TRUE becomes 1 вҖ” all numeric

[1] 1 2 1

c(1L, 2L, 3.14)       # integer вҶ’ double

[1] 1.00 2.00 3.14


RвҖҷs rule: when mixing types in one vector, coerce up to the most flexible type (character > double > integer > logical).

title: Data in R
text:
One type per vector
An atomic vector is homogeneous: every element must be of the same type вҖ” all double, or all integer, or all character, or all logical, etc.

typeof(c(1.5, 2.7, 3.0))          # "double"

[1] "double"

typeof(c(1L, 2L, 3L))             # "integer"

[1] "integer"

typeof(c("GC", "TF", "LZ"))       # "character"

[1] "character"

typeof(c(TRUE, FALSE, TRUE))      # "logical"

[1] "logical"








Important


Mixing types is not forbidden вҖ” R silently coerces everything to one common type (see Coercion gotchas below), which is almost never what you want. If you need several types in one object, use a data frame: one column per type.

title: Data in R
text:
Creating vectors

# combine вҖ” the main way to build a vector
nights  <- c(2, 5, 7, 10, 14)
islands <- c("Gran Canaria", "Tenerife", "Lanzarote",
             "Fuerteventura", "La Palma")

# sequences
1:5

[1] 1 2 3 4 5

seq(from = 0, to = 1, by = 0.25)

[1] 0.00 0.25 0.50 0.75 1.00

rep("GC", times = 3)

[1] "GC" "GC" "GC"

length(nights)

[1] 5

title: Data in R
text:
Vectorised arithmetic

price   <- c(82, 95, 110, 100, 78)
revenue <- nights * price                 # element-wise вҖ” no loops
revenue

[1]  164  475  770 1000 1092

mean(revenue)

[1] 700.2

sum(revenue)

[1] 3501

range(revenue)       # min and max

[1]  164 1092


This is the point of R. Whenever you are tempted to write a for loop, ask yourself whether vectorisation solves it.

title: Data in R
text:
Subsetting vectors

nights

[1]  2  5  7 10 14

nights[1]             # first element вҖ” R is 1-indexed

[1] 2

nights[c(1, 3, 5)]    # several positions

[1]  2  7 14

nights[-1]            # everything EXCEPT the first

[1]  5  7 10 14

nights[nights > 5]    # logical subsetting вҖ” most useful

[1]  7 10 14


The logical form (x[condition]) is the one you will use 95 % of the time.

title: Data in R
text:
Named vectors

price <- c(
  "Gran Canaria"  = 82,
  "Tenerife"      = 95,
  "Lanzarote"     = 110,
  "Fuerteventura" = 100,
  "La Palma"      = 78
)

price["Tenerife"]

Tenerife 
      95 

price[price > 90]

     Tenerife     Lanzarote Fuerteventura 
           95           110           100 


Named vectors are a stepping stone to data frames.

title: Data in R
text:
The rectangular data model

library(tibble)

hotels <- tibble(
  island = c("Gran Canaria", "Tenerife", "Lanzarote",
             "Fuerteventura", "La Palma"),
  stars  = c(4L, 5L, 4L, 3L, 3L),
  price  = c(82, 95, 110, 100, 78),
  nights = c(12.5, 18.3, 9.8, 11.2, 6.4)
)

hotels

# A tibble: 5 Г— 4
  island        stars price nights
  <chr>         <int> <dbl>  <dbl>
1 Gran Canaria      4    82   12.5
2 Tenerife          5    95   18.3
3 Lanzarote         4   110    9.8
4 Fuerteventura     3   100   11.2
5 La Palma          3    78    6.4



Rows = observations (one hotel per row).
Columns = variables (each has one type).
Every column has the same length.

title: Data in R
text:
Inspecting a data frame

dim(hotels)      # rows, columns

[1] 5 4

nrow(hotels)

[1] 5

ncol(hotels)

[1] 4

names(hotels)

[1] "island" "stars"  "price"  "nights"



head(hotels)     # first 6 rows
tail(hotels)     # last 6 rows
glimpse(hotels)  # tidyverse вҖ” compact overview
summary(hotels)  # base R вҖ” stats per column
View(hotels)     # spreadsheet view (RStudio)

title: Data in R
text:
Selecting columns

hotels$price           # dollar notation вҖ” a single column as a vector

[1]  82  95 110 100  78

hotels[["price"]]      # equivalent, bracket form

[1]  82  95 110 100  78

hotels[, "price"]      # still a one-column tibble

# A tibble: 5 Г— 1
  price
  <dbl>
1    82
2    95
3   110
4   100
5    78

mean(hotels$price)

[1] 93

title: Data in R
text:
Adding and modifying columns

hotels$revenue <- hotels$price * hotels$nights
hotels

# A tibble: 5 Г— 5
  island        stars price nights revenue
  <chr>         <int> <dbl>  <dbl>   <dbl>
1 Gran Canaria      4    82   12.5   1025 
2 Tenerife          5    95   18.3   1738.
3 Lanzarote         4   110    9.8   1078 
4 Fuerteventura     3   100   11.2   1120 
5 La Palma          3    78    6.4    499.


Later, with dplyr, weвҖҷll write this as:

library(dplyr)
hotels <- hotels |> mutate(revenue = price * nights)

title: Data in R
text:
Filtering rows

# base R
hotels[hotels$stars >= 4, ]

# A tibble: 3 Г— 5
  island       stars price nights revenue
  <chr>        <int> <dbl>  <dbl>   <dbl>
1 Gran Canaria     4    82   12.5   1025 
2 Tenerife         5    95   18.3   1738.
3 Lanzarote        4   110    9.8   1078 

# dplyr preview
library(dplyr)
hotels |> filter(stars >= 4)

# A tibble: 3 Г— 5
  island       stars price nights revenue
  <chr>        <int> <dbl>  <dbl>   <dbl>
1 Gran Canaria     4    82   12.5   1025 
2 Tenerife         5    95   18.3   1738.
3 Lanzarote        4   110    9.8   1078 


Pick one style and stick to it. WeвҖҷll standardise on dplyr from Data wrangling onwards.

title: Data in R
text:
The usual suspects



Format
Package
Function




CSV
readr
read_csv()


Excel (.xlsx, .xls)
readxl
read_excel()


SPSS (.sav)
haven
read_sav()


Stata (.dta)
haven
read_dta()


JSON
jsonlite
read_json()


Eurostat
eurostat
get_eurostat()



All of these are part of вҖ” or play nicely with вҖ” the tidyverse.

title: Data in R
text:
CSV with readr::read_csv

library(readr)
library(here)

occupancy <- read_csv(
  here("datasets", "raw", "istac-nights-by-island.csv"),
  locale = locale(decimal_mark = ",", grouping_mark = ".")
)

glimpse(occupancy)







Tip


Always go through here::here(). Your script will work on the lab machines, on your laptop, and on my laptop with zero changes.

title: Data in R
text:
Excel with readxl

library(readxl)

capacity <- read_excel(
  here("datasets", "raw", "istac-hotel-capacity.xlsx"),
  sheet = "2024",
  skip  = 3           # skip the first three header rows
)

glimpse(capacity)

Excel files often have merged cells, titles and footers. Use skip and range = "B5:K120" to carve out the actual data table.

title: Data in R
text:
SPSS and Stata with haven

library(haven)

survey <- read_sav(
  here("datasets", "raw", "tourism-survey.sav")
)

# haven preserves SPSS variable and value labels
attributes(survey$origin_country)

Use as_factor(survey) to convert labelled numeric variables into R factors in one go.

title: Data in R
section: Eurostat В· live from the API
text:
Eurostat В· live from the API

library(eurostat)
library(dplyr)

nights <- get_eurostat("tour_occ_nim", time_format = "date") |>
  filter(unit    == "NR",     # number of nights
         c_resid == "TOTAL",  # all residency statuses
         nace_r2 == "I551")   # hotels only

nights

The eurostat package caches downloads locally, so the slow step only happens the first time.

title: Data in R
text:
Reproducible paths with here::here()

library(here)

here()
# [1] "C:/Users/you/.../quantitative-methods-master-tides"

here("datasets", "raw", "eurostat-nights.csv")
# [1] "C:/Users/you/.../datasets/raw/eurostat-nights.csv"

here() always resolves relative to the project root (the folder with the .Rproj), regardless of which subfolder your .R or .qmd file lives in.

title: Data in R
text:
A first tourism pipeline

library(dplyr)
library(tibble)

hotels <- tribble(
  ~island,          ~month,  ~nights,  ~beds,
  "Gran Canaria",   "2024-06",  142500, 165000,
  "Gran Canaria",   "2024-07",  168300, 165000,
  "Tenerife",       "2024-06",  198200, 220000,
  "Tenerife",       "2024-07",  231100, 220000,
  "Lanzarote",      "2024-06",   88100, 105000,
  "Lanzarote",      "2024-07",   98400, 105000
)

hotels |>
  mutate(occupancy = nights / beds) |>
  group_by(island) |>
  summarise(mean_occupancy = mean(occupancy), .groups = "drop") |>
  arrange(desc(mean_occupancy))

# A tibble: 3 Г— 2
  island       mean_occupancy
  <chr>                 <dbl>
1 Tenerife              0.976
2 Gran Canaria          0.942
3 Lanzarote             0.888


This is essentially every report in tourism statistics: load В· clean В· group В· summarise. Tomorrow we unpack each verb.

title: Data in R
text:
Recap

R objects climb a ladder: atomic vectors вҶ’ named vectors вҶ’ data frames.
Learn the five types (numeric, character, logical, factor, Date) and you understand 90 % of what R prints.
Arithmetic on vectors is element-wise вҖ” avoid loops.
tibble / data.frame is the 99 %-of-the-time workhorse.
Import with the package that matches the file: readr, readxl, haven, eurostat.
Always use here::here() for paths.

title: Quantitative Methods in Tourism
text:
Weighting across modules






Important


Modules I and II combined count as 50 % of the subject mark. Module III accounts for the other 50 %.



So within the first half of the subject, each module weighs the same: Module I contributes 25 % and Module II contributes 25 % of the final subject mark.

title: Quantitative Methods in Tourism
text:
How you submit
Every in-class and final exercise is handed in via a Pull Request from your personal fork of the course repository on GitHub:

Fork https://github.com/chrglez/quantitative-methods-master-tides.
Clone your fork and branch off main at the start of every day.
Solve the template (.R for Days 1вҖ“5, .qmd for Day 6), commit and push the branch to your fork.
Open a Pull Request targeting chrglez:main with title Day N вҖ” Your Full Name.
I review with inline comments. PRs are never merged вҖ” they are the audit trail of your submission.

Campus Virtual is used only for announcements and the final grade. The full Git workflow is covered in the first hour of Day 1.

title: Quantitative Methods in Tourism
text:
Resources

Slides В· https://chrglez.github.io/quantitative-methods-master-tides/
Campus Virtual В· all announcements, exercise briefs and grades.
Office hours В· see top slide вҖ” email first.

title: Git & GitHub essentials
text:
Why version control?

Keep a full history of every change вҖ” who, what, when, why.
Collaborate without emailing analysis_final_v3_reallyfinal.R.
Revert safely when an experiment breaks your code.
Make your work reproducible and publicly auditable.







Important


In this course every exercise you submit lives on GitHub. The workflow has to be working for you by the end of this hour.

title: Git & GitHub essentials
text:
Git vs GitHub









Git
GitHub




What it is
A tool that tracks changes
A web service that hosts Git projects


Runs
Locally, on your laptop
On github.com


Needed for
Any version control at all
Sharing, collaboration, PRs


Cost
Free, open source
Free for public repos and education



You need both: Git installed locally, a GitHub account online.

title: Git & GitHub essentials
text:
The three Git areas
в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ   git add   в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ  git commit  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
в”Ӯ   Working   в”Ӯ в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв–¶ в”Ӯ   Staging   в”Ӯ в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв–¶ в”Ӯ  Local repo    в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  directory  в”Ӯ             в”Ӯ   (index)   в”Ӯ              в”Ӯ  (your laptop) в”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ             в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ              в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
                                                                  в”Ӯ
                                                         git push в”Ӯ
                                                                  в–ј
                                                         в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
                                                         в”Ӯ  GitHub remote в”Ӯ
                                                         в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
In this course you drive every arrow from RStudioвҖҷs Git pane, not from a terminal.

title: Git & GitHub essentials
text:
Step 1 В· Install Git

macOS В· brew install git, or install the Xcode Command Line Tools.
Windows В· download Git for Windows and accept the defaults.
Linux В· sudo apt install git (Debian/Ubuntu) or sudo dnf install git (Fedora).

After install, restart RStudio. It detects Git automatically.

title: Git & GitHub essentials
section: Step 2 В· Set your identity вҖ” no terminal
text:
Step 2 В· Set your identity вҖ” no terminal
From the R console:

usethis::use_git_config(
  user.name  = "Your Name",
  user.email = "you@ulpgc.es"
)

Every commit you ever make will be signed with this name and email. Use the same email you will register with on GitHub (next step).

title: Git & GitHub essentials
text:
Step 3 В· GitHub account + PAT
Create a GitHub account at https://github.com/signup with your ULPGC email. Apply for the free Student Developer Pack вҖ” private repos, Copilot and more.
Create a Personal Access Token (PAT) from R:

usethis::create_github_token(
  scopes      = c("repo", "user", "workflow"),
  description = "R on my laptop В· TIDES course",
  expiration  = 90                 # 90 days covers the whole course
)

The browser opens on GitHub. Leave the fields as prefilled, confirm 90 days, click Generate token. Copy the token immediately вҖ” GitHub never shows it again.

title: Git & GitHub essentials
text:
Step 3 В· Save the token
Back in R, paste the token into the credential store:

gitcreds::gitcreds_set()

title: Git & GitHub essentials
text:
Verify everything works

usethis::git_sitrep()

All rows should show вң… green. If anything is red, re-run the step the report points to.

title: Git & GitHub essentials
text:
Core vocabulary

Repository (repo) В· a project folder tracked by Git.
Commit В· a saved snapshot with a message.
Branch В· an independent line of development.
Remote В· a copy of the repo hosted elsewhere.
Fork В· your personal copy of someone elseвҖҷs repo on GitHub.
Pull Request (PR) В· a proposal to merge changes, with review.
Clone В· local download of a remote repo.
Push / Pull В· send / receive commits to / from a remote.
Sync fork В· one-click update of your fork from the original repo.

title: Git & GitHub essentials
text:
How everything flows

flowchart LR
  A[chrglez/<br/>course repo] -->|fork| B[yourname/<br/>course fork]
  B -->|clone| C[RStudio on<br/>your laptop]
  C -->|commit + push| B
  B -->|Pull Request| A
  A -->|review comments| B
  A -.->|Sync fork<br/>web button| B




flowchart LR
  A[chrglez/<br/>course repo] -->|fork| B[yourname/<br/>course fork]
  B -->|clone| C[RStudio on<br/>your laptop]
  C -->|commit + push| B
  B -->|Pull Request| A
  A -->|review comments| B
  A -.->|Sync fork<br/>web button| B






The dashed arrow is the Sync fork button on GitHub вҖ” thatвҖҷs how you pull course updates into your fork without a terminal.

title: Git & GitHub essentials
text:
The same story, in words

Fork the course repo on GitHub вҖ” one click, one time only.
Clone your fork from RStudio.
Each day: branch вҶ’ solve вҶ’ commit вҶ’ push.
Open a Pull Request against chrglez:main.
Christian reviews with inline comments. PRs are never merged.
If the course repo changed while you worked, click Sync fork on your forkвҖҷs page.

title: Git & GitHub essentials
section: Step 1 В· Fork the course repo
text:
Step 1 В· Fork the course repo
Go to https://github.com/chrglez/quantitative-methods-master-tides.
Click Fork (top-right). GitHub asks for a name вҖ” leave the default. Keep вҖңCopy the main branch onlyвҖқ ticked.
You land on your own copy: github.com/<your-user>/quantitative-methods-master-tides.

title: Git & GitHub essentials
section: Step 2 В· Clone your fork from RStudio
text:
Step 2 В· Clone your fork from RStudio
On your forkвҖҷs page, click Code вҶ’ HTTPS and copy the URL.
In RStudio:

File вҶ’ New Project вҶ’ Version Control вҶ’ Git.
Paste the URL into Repository URL.
Choose a stable parent folder (~/Documents or equivalent вҖ” not Desktop or OneDrive).
Click Create Project.

RStudio clones, opens the project, and the Git pane appears in the top-right.

title: Git & GitHub essentials
section: Step 3 В· Add the upstream remote
text:
Step 3 В· Add the upstream remote






Note


This is the only terminal command in the whole course.



Inside RStudio, open Tools вҶ’ Terminal вҶ’ New Terminal and paste:
git remote add upstream https://github.com/chrglez/quantitative-methods-master-tides.git
git remote -v
The second line should print two remotes: origin (your fork) and upstream (the course repo). After this, close the terminal вҖ” you wonвҖҷt need it again.

title: Git & GitHub essentials
section: вҡ пёҸ Never commit on main
text:
вҡ пёҸ Never commit on main
The main branch of your fork is a read-only mirror of the course repo. If you accidentally commit to it, syncing with upstream becomes painful.
Every day you work on a new branch, named after the day: day-1, day-2, вҖҰ day-6.
If in doubt, look at the branch selector at the top-right of the Git pane. It always tells you where you are.

title: Git & GitHub essentials
section: в‘  Sync your fork В· in the browser
text:
в‘  Sync your fork В· in the browser
Go to github.com/<your-user>/quantitative-methods-master-tides.
If Christian updated the course repo while you were away, a banner appears:

This branch is N commits behind chrglez:main В· Sync fork в–ј

Click it вҶ’ Update branch. Done, one click.
Then in RStudioвҖҷs Git pane click Pull (blue down arrow) to bring those commits to your laptop.

title: Git & GitHub essentials
section: в‘Ў Start a branch for the day В· Git pane
text:
в‘Ў Start a branch for the day В· Git pane
In the Git pane (top-right of RStudio):

Click the branch dropdown вҶ’ New BranchвҖҰ
Name it day-1 (or day-N).
Leave Remote: origin and tick Sync branch with remote.
Click Create.

You are now on day-1, main is safe.

title: Git & GitHub essentials
text:
в‘ў Solve the exercise
Open exercises/day1/exercise-template.R. Fill in your solutions using what we covered in Data in R. Save (Ctrl + S).
Run the script top-to-bottom (Ctrl + Shift + Enter) to confirm it runs without errors.

title: Git & GitHub essentials
text:
в‘Ј Commit В· Git pane
In the Git pane:

Tick the checkbox next to exercises/day1/exercise-template.R.
Click Commit.
A dialog opens. Write a message: вҖңDay 1 solutionвҖқ.
Click Commit.

RStudio shows the green confirmation. Your commit exists locally.

title: Git & GitHub essentials
text:
в‘Ө Push В· Git pane
Click the green up arrow (Push) in the Git pane.
RStudio sends your branch to GitHub. The first time per branch it sets the upstream tracking automatically (because you ticked Sync branch with remote in step в‘Ў).

title: Git & GitHub essentials
section: в‘Ҙ Open the Pull Request В· in the browser
text:
в‘Ҙ Open the Pull Request В· in the browser
Go back to your fork on GitHub. A yellow banner appears:

day-1 had recent pushes В· [ Compare & pull request ]

Click it. The PR form opens with the right defaults:

base В· chrglez/quantitative-methods-master-tides, branch main
compare В· <your-user>/quantitative-methods-master-tides, branch day-1

Title: Day 1 вҖ” Your Full Name. Click Create pull request.

title: Git & GitHub essentials
text:
ThatвҖҷs the whole loop
You have submitted. Christian receives a notification, reads the PR, leaves inline comments, and eventually closes it.
For the next day, start again from в‘  Sync your fork.

title: Git & GitHub essentials
text:
Commit messages that donвҖҷt suck


Bad
asdf
update
fix
final version
ok

Good
Day 1 solution
Add summary table by island
Fix NA handling in occupancy_rate
Rename variable for clarity
Imperative mood, starts with a verb, вүӨ 50 chars.

title: Git & GitHub essentials
text:
Common pitfalls

Never commit secrets вҖ” .Rdata, tokens, credentials. The .gitignore already excludes most traps.
Never commit on main вҖ” branches exist for this.
First Push вҖ” RStudio handles the upstream tracking automatically if you ticked Sync branch with remote when creating the branch.
Authentication failed? Your PAT may have expired. Run usethis::create_github_token(expiration = 90) and gitcreds::gitcreds_set() again.
Large files (>50 MB) donвҖҷt belong in Git. Put them under datasets/raw/ (gitignored) and commit the download script.

title: Git & GitHub essentials
text:
Where to ask for help

Happy Git with R В· Jenny BryanвҖҷs canonical guide.
GitHub Skills В· short interactive tracks.
Inside this repo В· exercises/README.md, tutorial hours on the welcome deck.

title: Basic statistics with R
text:
Outline

Univariate descriptives: mean, median, variance, quantiles.
Frequency tables and cross-tabulations.
Correlation and covariance.
Inference 101: t-test, chi-squared, proportion test.
Communicating results: broom::tidy() and nicely formatted tables.

title: Basic statistics with R
text:
Descriptives in one line

library(dplyr)

hotels |>
  summarise(
    n        = n(),
    mean     = mean(price, na.rm = TRUE),
    sd       = sd(price,   na.rm = TRUE),
    median   = median(price, na.rm = TRUE),
    q25      = quantile(price, 0.25, na.rm = TRUE),
    q75      = quantile(price, 0.75, na.rm = TRUE)
  )

title: Basic statistics with R
Basic statistics with R
Basic statistics with R
Basic statistics with R
section: A simple t-test В· does price differ between two islands?
text:
A simple t-test В· does price differ between two islands?

t.test(price ~ island,
       data   = filter(hotels, island %in% c("Gran Canaria", "Tenerife")))

title: Basic statistics with R
text:
Tidy inference with broom

library(broom)

t.test(price ~ island,
       data = filter(hotels, island %in% c("Gran Canaria", "Tenerife"))) |>
  tidy()

title: Basic statistics with R
text:
Recap

summarise() + a handful of functions covers most univariate reporting.
janitor::tabyl() beats base table() for presentable frequency tables.
broom::tidy() converts any test into a one-row data frame.

title: Multi-variable visualisation
text:
Outline

Mapping aesthetics: x, y, colour, size, shape, alpha.
Two continuous variables: scatter, with smoothers.
One continuous + one categorical: side-by-side distributions.
Facets: small multiples.
Correlation matrices and heatmaps.
Mapping tourism data to Canary Islands geography.

title: Multi-variable visualisation
text:
Scatter В· price vs rating

library(ggplot2)

ggplot(hotels, aes(x = rating, y = price, colour = island)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.7) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE) +
  labs(x = "Rating (1вҖ“10)", y = "Nightly price (вӮ¬)") +
  theme_minimal()

title: Multi-variable visualisation
section: Facets В· one panel per island
text:
Facets В· one panel per island

ggplot(hotels, aes(x = nights, y = price)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.5) +
  facet_wrap(~ island, ncol = 4) +
  theme_minimal()

title: Multi-variable visualisation
section: Categorical Г— continuous В· violin + boxplot
text:
Categorical Г— continuous В· violin + boxplot

ggplot(hotels, aes(x = island, y = price)) +
  geom_violin(fill = "#f4f3ee", linewidth = 0.3) +
  geom_boxplot(width = 0.2, fill = "#0067a2", colour = "white") +
  coord_flip() +
  theme_minimal()

title: Multi-variable visualisation
Multi-variable visualisation
section: Maps В· ISTAC data on Canary Islands
text:
Maps В· ISTAC data on Canary Islands

library(sf)
library(ggplot2)

# Shape file for Canary Islands municipalities (download separately).
muni <- read_sf("datasets/raw/canarias-municipios.geojson")

muni |>
  left_join(occupancy_by_muni, by = "muni_code") |>
  ggplot() +
  geom_sf(aes(fill = nights_per_capita), colour = "white", linewidth = 0.2) +
  scale_fill_viridis_c() +
  theme_void()

title: Multi-variable visualisation
text:
Principles

One story per plot. If it takes a paragraph to read, split it.
Colour should encode one variable at a time.
Prefer facets over a forest of overlaid lines.
Check for overplotting вҖ” use alpha, jitter, or 2D binning.
